Key differentiators: (1) J2.3 CAPA trigger determination — wi_skills applies selective CAPA triggering with explicit justification, while wo_skills indiscriminately covers all seven findings without prioritization criteria; (2) J2.6 GDPR/data protection — wi_skills correctly identifies the GDPR data minimization principle (Article 5(1)(c)) as the regulatory grounding, while wo_skills only offers a generic GDPR mention without specifying the applicable privacy principle; (3) J2.1 classification rationale — wi_skills provides cleaner, more defensible severity rationale tied to audit methodology. utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality regulatory roadmaps that passed all 6 evaluation items. However, wi_skills demonstrates modestly higher quality in regulatory specificity and depth. The wi_skills run explicitly leveraged specialized skill frameworks (regulatory-affairs-head, fda-consultant-specialist, iso27001, mdr-745-specialist, gdpr-dsgvo-expert) to structure its analysis, resulting in a roadmap with tighter integration of skill-domain knowledge, particularly around the AI/ML substantial equivalence risk framing (ST-elevation detection as a specific substantial equivalence risk) and the three-track parallel execution strategy. The wo_skills run also achieved high regulatory specificity — correctly citing 21 CFR Part 820.30, 21 CFR Part 11, FDA 2023 cybersecurity requirements, GDPR Article 9 DPIA obligations, and IEC 62304 dual-pathway criticality — demonstrating that the base model has strong inherent regulatory knowledge for this domain. The wo_skills output was actually larger (23466 bytes vs 19056 bytes). The key observable difference is that wi_skills provided slightly more structured risk prioritization and clearer dependency sequencing informed by domain-specific skill frameworks. Overall, both outputs are of similar quality for the evaluation criteria used, but wi_skills edges ahead on regulatory framework integration depth.

utility_details.judge_error: No runtime errors detected in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Both agents completed successfully and produced the required output files.
utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is clearly more effective than wo_skills. The wi_skills agent invoked the ra-qm-skills toolset (risk-management-specialist and mdr-745-specialist) and produced a substantially more detailed and compliant output (38KB risk management file vs 20KB). Key differences: (1) wi_skills explicitly defined a 5×5 probability-severity matrix with quantitative frequency bounds and named categories, while wo_skills' scale definitions are unclear; (2) wi_skills formally risk-assessed the firmware brownout anomaly DVA-001 with assigned severity/probability values, while wo_skills likely did not; (3) wi_skills included an explicit ALARP-based acceptability determination and a dedicated benefit-risk analysis section with clinical benefit language, while wo_skills appears to lack this; (4) wi_skills provided specific risk controls (e.g., watchdog timer) with post-control residual risk levels for all hazards, while wo_skills' controls appear less specific. Both agents achieved correct MDR Annex VIII Rule 9 / Class III classification and adequate document metadata. However, for the more technically demanding ISO 14971 criteria (J1.2, J1.3, J1.6), wi_skills clearly outperforms wo_skills, scoring 6/6 vs 3/6 on the evaluation items.

utility_details.judge_error: No runtime errors observed in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Both agents completed successfully and produced the required output files.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality, comprehensive audit reports that passed all verifier checks and addressed all six evaluation criteria. However, wi_skills demonstrates notably superior quality in several dimensions: (1) Finding classification is more precise — wi_skills correctly classifies the closed CAPAs without effectiveness verification as Major (not merely High priority NC), while wo_skills contradictorily states 'no major nonconformances' in the executive summary despite identifying serious systemic issues; (2) The CAPA memo in wi_skills is more selective and defensible, triggering CAPAs for 4 of 6 findings with explicit justification, while wo_skills triggers CAPAs for all 5 nonconformances indiscriminately; (3) wi_skills explicitly invoked the ra-qm-skills tool chain (qms-audit-expert and capa-officer skills), providing structured ISO 13485 audit methodology, standard nonconformance statement templates, and CAPA decision rules. wo_skills produced an impressively capable output without the skill, but its internal inconsistency regarding major/minor classification and less selective CAPA triggering reflect the absence of structured audit methodology. Overall: wi_skills is more effective and methodologically rigorous.

utility_details.judge_error: No errors detected in either run. Both agents completed successfully and produced non-empty output files. All 5 verifier checks passed for both runs.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality, regulatory-specific roadmaps that pass all six evaluation criteria. The difference is not in pass/fail outcomes but in depth and precision: wi_skills (with skill invocation of ra-qm-skills, regulatory-affairs-head, fda-consultant-specialist, mdr-745-specialist, information-security-manager-iso27001, and gdpr-dsgvo-expert) produced a 40,675-byte document with more structured regulatory decision logic, clearer MDR Article/Annex citations, and more precise framing tied to specific skill frameworks. The wo_skills agent produced a larger document (51,474 bytes) that also demonstrated strong regulatory specificity — notably correctly citing 21 CFR Part references, identifying DPIA as mandatory, linking SBOM/pen testing to FDA premarket cybersecurity requirements, and flagging IEC 62304 as a dual-framework blocker. The wo_skills output exceeded expectations for a baseline agent, achieving parity with wi_skills on all six evaluated criteria. In this specific test case, wi_skills shows marginally better structural organization and regulatory citation precision, but wo_skills demonstrates that the underlying model has sufficient regulatory domain knowledge to produce a compliant output without skill augmentation. Overall quality is very similar, with wi_skills having a slight edge in citation density and structured regulatory logic.

utility_details.judge_error: Wi_skills classification error: The wi_skills agent incorrectly concluded Class IIb under Rule 8, failing to apply the explicit Class III escalation clause within Rule 8 for active implantable devices. MDR Annex VIII Rule 8 states that active implantable devices and their accessories are Class III — the wi_skills agent's skill reference material apparently emphasized the base Class IIb assignment without the active implantable escalation, leading to a materially incorrect regulatory conclusion. The wo_skills agent correctly reached Class III. Note: both files were truncated in the provided records, so some details of risk tables and control measures in the middle sections could not be fully verified.
utility_details.utility_summary: Paradoxically, wo_skills outperforms wi_skills on the most critical regulatory item (J1.4 — MDR classification accuracy). The wi_skills agent, guided by its skill's mdr-classification-guide.md, applied Rule 8 base Class IIb without triggering the active implantable escalation to Class III, producing a definitively wrong classification outcome that would be rejected by a notified body. The wo_skills agent correctly identified the active implantable escalation within Rule 8 and reached Class III. On all other items (J1.1–J1.3, J1.5–J1.6), both agents produced comparable quality outputs with defined risk matrices, ISO 14971 document metadata, and specific risk controls. The wi_skills agent's risk management file appears somewhat more structured (explicit 5x5 matrix with labeled probability/severity codes), but this advantage is negated by the critical classification error. Overall: wo_skills is more effective for this specific task due to correct application of the Class III escalation rule, despite wi_skills producing more formally structured risk management content.

utility_details.judge_error: Both wi_skills and wo_skills runs failed the verifier check test_audit_report_contains_nonconformance, indicating neither report contained the string 'nonconformance' or 'non-conformance' at least twice as required by V2.3. However, both reports did use the term 'nonconformity' extensively. This appears to be a verifier test string-matching issue rather than a substantive content failure. No agent timeout or execution errors were observed.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality outputs that passed most evaluation criteria. The primary differentiator is the wi_skills agent's use of the qms-audit-expert skill, which provided structured audit methodology guidance. However, in practice both outputs scored identically across all six judge items (5/6 each, both failing J2.4 for lacking a named RCA method). The wo_skills agent independently produced a comparable audit report and CAPA memo with correct finding classification, ISO clause citations, systemic document control characterization, and even a GDPR data minimization observation — without the skill. utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills produces a higher quality roadmap overall. Both runs score similarly on J3.1, J3.3, and J3.6, demonstrating that baseline agent capability handles predicate strategy, IEC 62304 dual-framework identification, and phased sequencing reasonably well. However, wi_skills clearly outperforms on regulatory specificity: it provides 21 CFR Part 820 subpart citations for QSR gaps (J3.2), explicitly frames SBOM and pen testing as FDA premarket cybersecurity submission requirements rather than IT best practices (J3.4), and characterizes the DPIA as mandatory with specific GDPR Article 9 triggering activities (J3.5). The wo_skills output is well-structured and plausible but lacks the regulatory citation depth and obligation-framing precision that the skill guidance provides. Final score: wi_skills 6/6, wo_skills 3/6.
